% CURRENT AFFAIRS,

Members of the Stock Exchange have their own

5 phraseology. Last week the financial column in one of
: the daily papers described Great Southern Ordinary
Stock as “sick.”” What does the term mean? A few
years ago the Great Southern Ordinary Stock was 147.

At the date of the last half-yearly meeting, 12th August,
1905, it was a fraction more than 92. At the begin-

i ning of last week it was a little over 90, and on Thurs-
= day it suddenly fell to 89. Friday’s report was eagerly
5 Jooked for, and on Friday, to the dismay of sellers and
holders, the Ordinary Stock fell another point, that is
‘to 88, and was said to be “sick.”” The total amount of
Ordinary Stock is £5,326,620, so that a fall of one

. point means a loss to shareholders of over £53,000. In
two days of last week the property of the Great Southern
ghareholders had, therefore, depreciated by over
£106,000. . Stock Exchange experts, using their own
~ terms, may doscribe this state of things by saying that
‘the Stock in question was “sick;’’ we, not being ox-
~ perts, feel disposed to think that the shareholders must

be “sick ’—sick of the management under which, in
seven years, the shares have fallen from 147 to 88, where
cach 'point means a loss of over £53,000.

‘What is the need of dances or dinners in the Mansion
House, Dublin, at the public expense? We can under-
stand tho Lord Mayor of Dublin entertaining certain
classes of distinguished visitors to the city, but we can-
not see why the ratepayers’ money should be spent in
giving free entertainments to a clique, or collection of
cliques, of local people, probably largely composed of
the Johnnie type. A large ratepayer informed us the
other day that, within his knowledge, three large rate-
payers desired some years ago to secure for themselves
and a few relations and friends an invitation to some
Mansion House entertainments, but they did not suc-
ceed. Our informants’ disappointment was all the more
keen when he heard that youngsters from lodgings who
never paid a penny rates in their lives were bounding
about in the Mansion House.

The present Lord Mayor took a second year of office
at a reduced salary, and a contract is a contract. If
the Lord Mayor, a man of limited means, found by ex-
perience that he had done a financially foolish thing in
contracting to serve as Lord Mayor on the reduced
terms, and if he took the Corporation into his confi-
dence, and showed that the contract he had blindly en-
tered into would land him into a personal liability of a
certain sum of money, there might be many in favour
of voting him such an extra sum as would insure him
against actual financial loss. Future Lord Mayors could
be told that in their cases the bond would be strictly
adhered to. But the sensational proposal sprung upon
the Dublin rate-paying public recently has no justifica-
tion. It was altogether a clumsy, as well as a bare-
faced, piece of business.

Recently a correspondent asked us, and we, in turn,
asked our readers, for some advice concerning the work-
ing of Temperance vans. Well, we have received some
information as a consequence of our request concerning
the working not of a Temperance van, but of a coffee-
house in the West of Ireland. About four years ago it
was decided to open a coffee-house on the Fair Green, at
Dromore West, Sligo. A convenient site was happily
obtained at a nominal rent of 1d. a year, and the coffee-
house was erccted by subscription. It has been open for
the monthly fairs, for the sports. and agricultural shows.
The aim of those conducting it was, and is, to provide
good food at the lowest possible prices. A good fire on
a bitter winter’s day is a great attraction as well as a hot
meal.. The charges are:—

Tea, coffee, or cocoa, per large cup ... Id.
Large currant bun ... oo Gl
Three slices bread e dy
Butter or jam soo ek
Eggs, each sao gl
Ham, per {lb. plate ool

These prices allow of a man having a good meal for 5d.
or 6d. Tho conductors of the Dromore West Coffee-
house are able to make ends meet at these prices, but,
needless to say, very careful and economic management
ig necessary. The baker allows 2d. in the Is. on the
bread, and takes back the surplus. Large numbers
avail thomsclves of the coffee-house, and at big fairs
there is gonerally a steady stream of customers during
six or seven hours.

‘Why could not an enterprise of this kind be estab-
lished in hundreds of other villages as well as Dromore
West, Sligo? This points a way for useful, constructive

“Nationalists > and “Idolators.””

work in the Temperance Cause. Might we suggost that
portion of the proceeds of the All-Ireland Temperance
Bazaar could not be better employed than in aiding ven-
tures of this kind all over the country.

T}.\e Commissioners of * National’” Education, in
their report for 1904, state: that, ¢ on the initiation of
the teachers, followed by the managers, an agitation
sprang up ”’ against the now notorious Rule 127 (b).
These Dublin Castle eclected Commissioners set out
several of the objections that have been urged against
this notorious Rule. Here is one :— (f) That, coupled
with Rule 186, it would lead to the amalgamation of
boys and girls’ schools and the consequent co-oducation
of the sexes—a consequence fraught with grave moral
danger.”

The Commissioners selected by Dublin Castle reply :
—“ With regard to the fact of the education of boys
and girls in the same school, sentimental traditions have
faken place of fact and experience.  The danger of
abuse seems very remote at a primary school which boys
and girls attend. They sit at different desks and have
different playgrounds.  They never associate during
school hours. If there is any danger—and in Ireland
the danger should be very slight on account of the
strict supervision exercised by the clergy—it is much
greater when the children associate, as they do, on
their way home from school.” Thus the Castle-

: oners brush aside the deep and
abiding Catholic objection to mixed schools as an airy
thing of ‘‘ sentimental tradition.”

It is obvious that the great necessary reform is the
sweeping away of this nominated Dublin Castle Board.
There are no two questions about that. How to do it—
is the problem. As far as we can see, one thing that
can be done is that all the popularly elected Boards in
the country could make themselves as nasty as possible
to all the nominated Castle Boards of all kinds—Edu-
cational, Local Government, and the rest. Down in
New Ross Mr. John Cummins is in rebellion against the
Local Government Board. Isolated attempts can easily
be beaten, but a general adoption of a rebellious spirit
on the part of all the Elected Boards and other bodies
or individuals having any opportunity of making them-
selves felt against the whole gamut of the nominated
Castle Boards will soon bring about an effective situa-
tion. If the ““ National ”’ Board is to be swept away,
all the nominated Dublin Castle Boards must be at-
tacked with any and all sorts of weapons that are
effective, and that are calculated to do a maximum of
damage to the Dublin Castle Boards, and a minimum
amount of damage to the popular bodies. Speeches and
resolutions and newspaper articles are no use as weapons
except in so far as they inspire a public resolve that in
its turn will lead to actions that are effective. Trinity
College, though not a Castle Board, is one of the Castle’s
brats, and it deserves all that it is possible to give it.

Rochfort Bridge is called after one Rochford—in his
day, one of the greatest Priest-hunters in Westmeath.
There was a concert there recently in aid of funds to
defray the cost of repairs to the local Protestant school.
There was not one single item in Irish on the pro-
gramme or a single Irish song sung at the entertain-
ment.  But the stage-Irishman was there; English
flags hung round the walls of the schoolroom, and the
English National Anthem, which in this country means,
for the most part, down with the Nationalists and
“Idolators,”” was chanted at the close of the porfor-
mance. There was a large audience, but those belong-
ing to England’s Faithful Garrison in this country could
be accommodated on a few side-cars; the majority were
No wonder the
numerical majority is a practical minority!

The results of the Intermediate Examinations.
throw some wholesome light on Irish as a secondary
school study. Its study certainly did not appear in any
way to handicap St. Colman’s College, Fermoy, in 1?9
healthy rivalry with other Colleges. ~ St. Vincent’s
College, Castleknock, is, unhappily, not _notcd for_ its
enthusiastic support of Irish, and yet in comparison
with St. Colman’s vecord at the last Intermediate
Examinations it is, to say the least, difficult to discern
where the advantage of giving Irish the cold shoulder
manifests itself. The College Ass is dead, but his long-
cared spirit goes marching on.

St. Colman’s passed five of its Senior Grade students
in Trish, eighteen in the Middle Grade, thirty-eight in
the Junior, and nine in the Preparatory—the total
number of Irish passes in all Grades was no less than
seventy-one, and of these thirty-three passed with
Honours.  Yet, notwithstanding this—West Britons
might not unprofitably ask themselves, was it largely
because of it—St. Colman’s students have reflected
very high honour, indeed, upon their College.  They
have taken nine exhibitions, and the student who se-
cured first place in the Middle Grade likewise won a
composition prize in Irish, and one of the Junior Exhi-
bitioners took tenth place in Irish in the Grade.

The Intermediate results show that a large number
of clever Nationalist Papist youths are yearly turned
out seeking for a market for their skill and abilities.
If we had the control of our own education, we would,
no doubt, so order it that it would tend to serve the
living needs of the country in its present state of de-
velopment. However, the fact is that the Intermediate
system that is forced upon the country turns out thou-
sands of clever young “ Idolators’’ fit to be competent
clerks. But what chances have they of getting a start
in the Bank of Ireland, in the Great Northern Railway,
in the Provincial Bank? The Government have prac-
tically enacted that the Secondary schools turn out,
amongst others, thousands of young fellows fit to be
capablo clerks, and bigotry and ascendancy pop up their
brazen faces behind the tills of the Provincial Bank, the
dosks of the Great Northern Railway offices and other
places, and tell the qualified “ Idolatrous ”’ applicants
for work to go to Hell or out of the country. Great
struggles are complex things, and a band of determined
men who would fight anti-Irish bigotry at the meetings
of the Great Northern Railway or the Provincial Bank,
would be component parts of the army that fight the
Castlo Boards, including the nominated Dublin Castle
Commissionors of “National”” Education, who would
rob the Trish Language Movement of the help given to
if ‘by the fees paid for teaching it as an extra subject.

A romarkablo fact in connection with the splendid
results of 8f. Louis Convent, Carrickmacross, is that
100 per cent. of the pupils entered for the Intermediate
passed, and overy student passed in Irish.

As the

thorities of the Convent announce:—“The aim of

The publication of the Honours Results only is not
fair to numbers of small schools throughout the coun-
try that are doing good work with pass pupils, and that
have not stafis large enough to admit of separate
classes for Honour pupils. Of course, the Honours lists

of the declining Parochial University—one of Dublin
Castle’s brats.

DO AS ROME DOES.

““ That only one exhibition in the Senior Grade (girls)
out of thirteen awarded, should have been gained by
@ Protestant student, and that by a pupil of Rochelle
Seminary, Cork, is a fact which needs no comment of
ours to point a moral; and in the Middle Grade things
are little better.”’—Irisa TIMES.

< Thirty-seven exhibitions were given (Modern
Literary Course, Junior Grade), of which no less than
7 z have gone to Roman Catholic schools. This
£ is a very surprising one, and will, no doubt, give
rise to considerable searchings of heart in the Protestant
schools.”’—IrisE TIMES.

Ye seats of knowledge and of light,
Around the great ‘‘ Sahara ’’ scattered,
Your fame is in a ‘sorry plight
By dogma-fettered people shattered.

Oh, shame to think we are surpassed
Tn mental gifts by Papists slighted,
And our professors too out-classed
By Romish teachers so benighted.

What is the cause, the reason why,
Our prestige now so sadly suffers?
And we that thought we stood so high

Have turned out such awful duffers.

Whate’er the cause we have heen whacked
By Papists long from us so shrinking;
Good gracious, could it be a fact
That we, not they, are proxy-thinking?

Wake up, ye schools, and take your cue
From rampant Rome’s obscuring mission ;
Awake and take a tip or two
From priesteraft and from superstition.
Their methods study and mayhap
You soon may mend your education.
‘Tis sad to wear a dunce’s cap
And be a laugh for all the nation.

Ves, on their model let us start,
To keep abreast this great upheaval,
And coming times may find us smart
As proxy-thinkers medizyval.
AM.W.

Wo take the following from an editorial article in the
Irish Times of September 13th : —“It will be a surprise
to many to learn that the number of children receiving
what ab all events purports to be Intermediate Educa-
tion is proportione,tely larger in Ireland than it is in

~ England, and the proportion receiving such education
is suhstantially greater in Leinster and Munster than it

is in Ulster. Although there are 209,000 children be-

tween the ages of 5 and 18 in Ulster, there are only
4,641 on the rolls of Intermediate Schools. In Mun-
ster, with only 149,000 such children, there are 9,003
on the rolls of the same class of schools, and in Lein-
ster, with 145,000, there are no less than 12,286. In:
Belfast only 5 por thousand of the children are in
superior schools, while in Dublin, Cork, and Limerick.
there are 20, 26, and 28 per cent. respectively.”” No
wonder the authorities of the Great Northern Railway
are not anxious to throw open their clerkships to com-
petitive oxamination. According to these figures 21,286
pupils are receiving Intermediate Education in be-
nighted Muuster and Leinster as against 4,541 in
Ulster, where the “ civic virtue ’’ comes from.

Deipe-Peap  (Mr. J. J. Doyle) writing in the Derry
People, says:—“The Philosoply of Irish Ireland is a
reprint of a series of articles which appeared in 7'he New
Ireland Review from the pen of Mr., D. P. Moran, now
editor of the Leaber, in 1899-90. They are a most
powerful plea for the revival of our native language.
Those who cannot spare time to learn Irish, could not do
better than invest a shilling in Zhe Philosoply of Irish
Ireland, read it, and lend it to their friends.” Inisfail,.
the official organ of the Gaelic League in London, says—
“Myr. Moran’s Philosophy consists of articles reprinted
from Zhe New Ireland Review. They had very consider-
able effect at the period of their first appearance; Mr.
Moran was a bracing and sturdy pioneer.  To-day,
when some of the theories have become ftruisms, they
are still bracing and effective. Many of us well in the
swing of the movement are apt to forget that there are
hundreds nominally in sympathy with it who do not
really understand it. The Philosophy will open their
eyes to advantage. There are still other types to whom.
it will bring a shock that may do them good.””

At thirty minutes past noon on Friday last we hap-
pened to be looking through one of the windows of our of-
fices, and we thought we saw a back view of the tall, an-
gular figure of William O’Brien disappearing up the hall
of No. 7. We could not swear that it was William, but
in a few minutes a gentleman in the front office, first
floor, of No. 7 started up like one who had received a
shock. We knew, then, that William’s voice must have
resounded on the landing, and that the staff was all of
a flutter. In another few minutes William appeared
at a window and surveyed No. 33. He had not time to
indulge in very prolonged reflections when he disap-
peared from view again.

Limerick people are naturally anxious to do honour
to “M.O.’R.” Limerick has just cause to be proud of
Dr. O’Riordan. At the public meeting last week, Lord
Emly said :—“ Dr. O’Riordan was called upon to fill a
confidential position which required an education of no
mean order, a man of letters, the ability of a statesman,
and the tact of a diplomat. Former Rectors of the Irish
College included such priests of the Church as Cardinals
Cullen and Moran, Dr. Kirby, a Limerick man, Bishop
of Ephesus, and Dr. Kelly, present Coadjutor Arch-
bishop of Sydney. The selection of Dr. O'Riordan was
not without special significance. His last work was a
sturdy vindication of the Irish clergy from the strangely
improper attack made upon them by Sir Horace Piun-
kett, and was now an Irish text book.”

Judge Adams, in the course of his speech, remarked :
—“They in Limerick were very proud that the choice
of the four Archbishops, ratified by the Holy Father,
had fallen upon a young Limerick curate, and that he
had been promoted to one of the greatest offices to
which an Irish priest could succeed. The Rectorship
of the Irish College was & tie between Rome and Treland.
He was the ambassadar of the Irish race at the foot of
the Holy See.”

Mr. Robert Gibson, a mon-Catholic, joined in the
tributes to “M.O’R.’s’’ worth. ‘“Protestants,” he
said, “owed a dobt of gratitude to him for his late
publication ;*’ and, he added, that some fellow-Protes-
tants told him that Dr. O’Riordan’s work had opened
their eyes as nothing else had done. After all, the

place for the twenty-second cleverest boy, and the eighth 
arithmetician of his age iu Ireland. ' Most of the 
scoffers would be ready to take chances for the work-
house, and get all the praise he was receiving. 
Of 
com·se, this was in the smaller school; if the hero 
migrated, as he often did to a larger one, he found the 
si;ancla.rcl of glorification had increased. 
Here there 
were domi-gocls, too, young men of haughty intelligence 
and far-extended ambitions; but they were not mere ex-
hibitioners. 
In a laud where hook-prizemen were 
looked on as idlers, notbiug but medallists and first place-
men were held of account. Yet if 0ne did succeed in 
reaching that coveted position, there was a yea.r's demi-
deification to be had to the full. 
First in bis grade to-
day, he would be first in his couutry to-morrow. 
It 
would merely be a question of the degree of his pa-
triotism whether he would be Lord Chrmcellor or Leader 
of the Irish P arty. But suppose the results went wrong, 
then there wa.s a horrid peripet-i,t ; there came a fall 
like that of Sejauus or Robespierre, and the erstwhile 
great ones of the earth bit the ignoble dust. 
T'o-day one reads the results in a calmer mood. 
One 
is more interested in general conclusions than in particu-
lar. 
But surely the most interesting thing of all is the 
mode of their reception; the way in which the avid 
theological statistician stays up all night to do sums 
with denominational conclusions. 
Surely there is no 
clearer demonstration of the extra.ordina.ry state of 
things in our country than the way in which· this battle 
of the religions begins at the early age of fourteen. 
" Auel every babe that's born into this world alive, 
Is either a little Liberal or a little Conservative .. " 
The Irish youth finds himself joining iu the deadly 
strife, and ranged under a religious banner when be is 
scarce out of the nursery. 
Nor is this iu any way a 
unique phenomenon. It is of a piece with the whole 
fabric of Irish existence. 
'l'he Catholic schoolboy is all 
along fighting for his life. The Catholic school is ex-
posed to daily attack, and were it not able to hold its 
own, itnd to show its superiority by plain, undeniable 
facts, by mere undebatable totals and averages, it would 
soon perish beneath the onslaughts made upon it. 
For this l'eason I do not regret that the results should 
now again be published, after a solemn decision to the 
opposite effect. 
Since secondary education is the basis 
of the fabric of intellectual society, it is of the greatest. 
importance to be able to show the preclomiua.11ce of 
Catholics in that field. 
,}i711at a striking difference there 
is, for instance, between the rewards of Catholics in tho 
open field of competitive examination aµd in th~se other 
directions-public and private-where influence tells I 
But whilst approving of the publication of results, one 
cannot equally approve of the action of the Interme-.

diate Board in suddenly changing their minds shortly
after they had resolved, as a great reform, never to pub-
lish again. It is but one of a number of points which
tend to show that the motion of educational bodies is,
for the most part, circular.  Science tells us, indeed,
that where you have two equal and opposite forces acting

on different sides of a body, the body will
uegb]xcl' advance nor retreat, bubt rotate. Now
this is" precisely the constitution of all our
teaching authorities in Ireland, and as a result

these educational “couples’ are capable of revolving
round a reform for ever. The National Board has re-
volved round “hand and eye.”” The Intermediate Board
—not entirely through its own fault—has made a cir-
cuit of iuspection, and is now doing Catherine wheels
round ‘“fixed authors” and the  publication of
results.”

I have lately heard a very sinister criticism of the In-
termediate in some of its nt dovelopments. A prac-
tical teacher of long experience has told me that the
Intermediate is being Trinityised, if I may used the ex-
pression. He says that in such a subject as Greek,

+ Trinity influence is being more and more felt to the ex-
clusion of Royal University influence, and he instances
the increased prominence given to Greek and Latin
verse, a subject not studied in the R.U.I., but of great
importance in T.C.D. As both Assistant-Commis-
sioners are Trinity men, and Trinity has a considerable
predominance on the Board, such a policy might be very
effectively attempted, and if attempted should be strongly
resisted. The schools preparing for Trinity have cer-
tainly made remarkable advances in the classical course
since the results were last published. I hope if there is
anything in this criticism the matter will carefully be
looked into.

With the publication of results I daresay all the old
questions about the Intermediate education will be
raised again. Isit “ mere cram,”” “ miseducation,’” and
the rest, all of which charges serve as ready excuses to
those who send their children to England, when, per-
haps, the real motive is a forlorn attempt to escape a
Cork accent. It seems to me that the case against the
system has at all times been immensely exaggerated.
There are probably very few good students either of
Trinity or the Royal, or any other University of note
who do not submit to the same cramming that the In-
termediate brings about. For cramming means either
(1) hard work; (2) silly and useless work ; or (3) superfi-
cial work. Only the latter is really objectionable, since
there are few ways of life in which a great deal of the
first and the second are not necessary. There are few Uni-
versity courses and few professions in which one must
not devote part of his own time to the diligent study of
nonsense, whether it be Greek emendation, the absurdi-
ties of physiological theorists, or the hair-splitting hum-
bug of Real Property Law. Asto superficial work,
however, I believe few hoys have ever really attained
success by that means. If they have it has been the fault
not of the examination system, but of individual, incom-
petent examiners. It is, indeed, marvellous what intel-
lectual havoc may be wrought by one idiot on an
examining Board. I remember, for instance, a criminal
of this nature who once asked the name of some utterly
insignificant historical personage. Next year every boy
secking high places made and learnt off a list of all the
insignificant names in the course. It was the only
thing to do.

Catholics should, in general, strive to maintain the
repute of the Intermediate, for it is the foundation of
Catholic education. They have, been as a whglo,
amazingly successful in these contests. At one time

such results wore derided. But when it was discovered -

that all the successful men both in T.C.D. and the
R.U.I. were Intermediate men, this had to be given up.
As Trinity now recognises the Intermediate to the full,
Catholics may, perhaps, come to belicve in the reality of
their successes. CHANEL.

As the LEADER is no stranger to Irish-Americans on
this side of the Continent, and your humble servant in
particular who, after an absence of seventcen years, 1S
just returned from a four months’ tour through the old
land, enjoying every moment of my time thex‘r;, npt the

Shodyism and frauds. An organ that is so much in-

sure, publish the following grievances, which many
Americans were subject to this summer (I mean the
luggage departments of the railways) :—“ I met many
travellers on board the steamship Baltic coming from
Queenstown, on the 10th of August, to New York vxfho
had their trunks go astray from time to time during
their stay in Ireland, causing them great inconvenience.
I could mention many names and cases, but will content
myself by only quoting three whom I know intimately.
Mr. Manning, who was staying in Clifden, is a wealthy
merchant from Oil City, Pittsburg, had his trunk doing
the West of Ireland for eight days though labelled to its
proper destination. A Mr. M‘Cracken, from Co. Down,
had to wait nine days in the North for his luggage,
which was also directed to its proper landing; but the
worst case of all was a Mr. Loftus, a wealthy Irish-
American from Philadelphia, who had to sail without
his luggage, which was put in train with him, labelled
“ Queenstown,” and to his surprise when he got there
found no boxes, and had to leave owing to business en-
gagements minus two truuks, but got an assurance they
would follow after him.””

You may ask is this a new grievance in the railways;
I answer it is not, as, when we were boys years ago,
going to our homes in all parts of Ireland from business
and Colleges, we were considered wonderful travellers
if we managed to get our luggage home with us.

How do the mistakes occur? a stranger may wonder ;
but it is easy to see how they occur. It is due entirely
to want of system in the head organisation. The
trunks are labelled all right, but at many of the stations
a crowd gathers round the luggage van, and everyone
who wants luggage points to his or her boxes, and trunks
are pitched on top of each other in the most irregular
way, mixing them upside down till the train moves out
on time, leaving many unfortunates minus their boxes.

I was a passenger in ss. Baltic, which reached New
York 16th of August. She carried twenty-seven hun-
dred passengers, and can easily accommodate three
thousand five hundred on that voyage; she had three
thousand mail bags, and seven thousand trunks; and
half an hour after landing in New York there was
neither a, passenger or box at Wharf. Every one of
these boxes during that time was taken from hold of
ship, also inspected by Custom House officers, and in
hands of owners for various parts of the Continent.

How was this doue}? The usual answer by system.
Everyone understanding what he had to do, and keld
responsible for it, not letting Tom, Jack, or Harry te
the hold of ship for his luggage. . v

Your name is asked when putting in trunks or bag-
gage at Liverpool or Queenstown. If it is Mun)h?,
they put a letter “ M '’ on box; Jones, a “J;’’ Wolfe,
a “W,” and so on—then at Wharf, which is, by no
means, very large in proportion to trafic.  Divisions:
are lottered from A to Z, and when Murphy wants his
luggage, he goes to “M,” Jones to “J,” and they in-
variably get them. .

In contra-distinction to this perfect order of system,
I was at Broadstone Depot one afternoon. At the hour
I was there, I am sure thqre were fully eleven porters
on the platform, and at times only about five passen-
gors. They seemed to turn their attention altogether
on two gentlemen who looked good for tips,”” and I

BELFAST AND BIGOTRY.—I.

A RETROSPECT.

““So that from that time forth the Lord began to
erect a mew tabernacle for himself in Ireland and
ospecially in the northern parts of it, and spread more
the curtains of his habitation.””—7%he Adair Manuscript.

OMING home on Thursday evenings from the city
I feel, as I read the LeADER, the people in the
carriage looking from me to it and from it back again
to me. I have never seen the paper with any of them,
but, unless by assuming that they read it, I can’t ac-
count for their looking at me so. Perhaps, they do in
private. In Belfast, to read a paper like the LEADEE
and to read it in public are two different things.
Whether these fellow-passengers of mine do read it,
however, or do not, their looking so at me has suggested
to me that I should write something in the paper about
Belfast. With the editor’s permission I shall do so
under the above heading.

It is a commonplace of criticism as regards Ireland to
say that Irish history has never yet been written.
There are certainly some points to clear up. One of
these points is—How came Ulster to be so much more
thoroughly ‘ planted ’’ than either Leinster or Mun-
ster? I don’t know that the Ulster Unionists them-
selves know very well why they are, who they are, or
what they are. For the benefit of any of them who
read the LEADER, as well as for the information of those
in the other provinces who have but hazy ideas as to
what the “Black North ’’ is made up of, I shall briefly
trace the development of the latter from the Plantation
down. The doing so may help the ‘ man in the street’”
to form an opinion as to the correctness of the LEADER’s
contention that there are two nations in Ireland. Also,
with the help of the figures, I shall have by and by to
quote in connection with my subject, it may help the
same man in the street to a decision as to the best way
for the Gael to face the present day situation.

The Flight of the Earls of Tyrone and Tirconnell in
1607 it was that gave England the opportunity for the
Plantation of Ulster in general, but the neighbourhood
of where Belfast now stands had been planted some
years earlier. Two Scotch adventurers named Hamil-
ton and Montgomery obtained by ‘‘ diplomacy’’ pos-
session of the greater part of North Down from Con
O’Neill.  How that county especially and Ulster
generally had been prepared for the coming of such
adventurers is described by Fynes Morryson. ‘ Now,”’
he writes, “‘because I have often made mention formerly
of destroying the Rebels’ corné and using all means to
famish them, let me by two or three examples show
the miserable cstate to which the Rebels were thereby
brought. Sir Arthur Chicester, Sir Richard Morryson,
and the other Commissioners of the Forces sent against
Bryan MacArt aforesaid, in their return homeward
saw a most horrible spectacle of three children (whereof
the eldest was not above ten yeeres old) all eating and
knawing with their teeth the entrails of their dead
mother upon whose flesh they had fed twenty dayes
past and having eaten all from the feete upward to the
bare bones, roasting it continually by a slow fire, were
now come to the eating of her-entralls in like sort
roasted yet not divided from the body being yet raw.
. ... And no spectacle was more frequent in the
Ditches of Townes and especially in wasted countries
than to see multitudes of these poore people dead with
their mouths all coloured greene by eating nottles, docks
and all things thoy could rend above the ground.””
Chichester himself, in his official despatches to Council,
refers to this plan of operations as ‘‘ ploughing and
breaking up the barborous Irish nation and sowing the
soil with the seeds of civility,’”” and describes how “ wee
did so vexe and trouble them (the Irish) that most of
the naturall inhabytantes begane to wearie of suche
daungors.”’

superiority.  But by the Coisde Gnotha clection, if it be
by voting (ballot o otherwise), we have at once a grada-
tion established, and all the evils of jealousy and emula-
tion let loose.  Dr. Kuno Meyer I beliove it who
first turned the minds of Irish students in the direction
of an Academy. He has, [ fear, much to answer for.
If T vemember rightly, he wont as far as to name six
members that the divector was chosen by lot, and
the six were Dr. Hyde, Father O’Leary, Father Din-
neen, Mr. John MacNeill, Miss O’Farrelly, and Dr.
O’Hickey. Tt seems obvious that Dr. Meyer’s idea of
an Academy is different from what is now contemplated,
and that he seemed to take propagandist work as a quali-
fication as well as literary genius and mature learning.

The present writer approached the consideration of
this question with much sympathy and an open mind,
but the more I examine it, the more helpless seems to
me the morass in which the entire scheme is destined to
land us. That an Academy of some kind might be reared
as the top storey of a gradually progressive examination
scheme already tested by experience, and well rooted in
the country, is a proposition that many persons would
accept.  But to begin to build the top storey before
even the stones are brought together for the rest of the
building is surely a novelty in architecture. The Ard-
is true, approved of such an institution. But
s probably did not discuss the question at
any great length, nor give themselves time to see the
difficulties, and even absurdities, into which it would
land them. The entire scheme was approved of by the
Ard-Fheis merely as a general recommendation to the
Coisde Gnotha. T trust the latter body will take a sen-
sible view of the case, and not persist in a scheme which
is calculated to do much mischief and liftle good. Let
them, of course, carry on their examinations as best they
can. Even in the ordinary course of things the final
examinations will not be reached for a few years; let
them defer the Academy portion of the scheme till then.
Much clear light will, meantime, have been thrown on
many questions connected with it.

In another article I hope to state what steps I con-
sider should be taken at the present time as regards this
slippery and dangerous question.

“Does it not, then, seem as futile to imagine that he

tude of the undiscovered will, throughout all time, re-
main to humble his pride. ~ Our children’s children
will still be gazing and marvelling at the starry hea-

With irresistible force that address of Professor Dar-
win’s awakens in the minds of Catholics whose only re-
source, when confronted with the theories of mate-
realism, was to say—" I believe, do Thou, O Lord, help
my unbelief,” the famous passage in the Theory of
Bthics known to every student of philosophy.

“Two things there are,”” says Kant, “which, the
oftener and more steadfastly we consider them, fill the
mind with an ever-new and ever-rising admiration and
reverence—the Starry Heavens above and the Moral
Law within.””

“ Of neither am I compelled to seek out the reality as
veiled in darkness, or only to conjecture the possibility
as beyond the hemisphere of knowledge. Both I con-
template lying clear before me, and I connect both im-
mediately with consciousness of existence. The one de-
parts from the place I occupy in the outer world of
sense—expands, beyond the bounds of imagination,
the connection of my body with worlds rising beyond
worlds, and systems blending into systems—and pro-
tends it into the illimitable times of their periodic
movement, its commencement, and perpetuity. The
other departs from my invisible self, from my per-
sonality, and represents me in a world truly infinite,
indeed, but whose infinity can be thrashed out only
by the intellect, and my connection with which, unlike
the fortuitous relation in which I stand to the world
of sense, I am compelled to recognise as universal and
necessary. In the one, the first view of the countless
multitude of words, annihilates, as it were, my import-
ance as an animal product, which, after a brief and
incomprehensible endowment with the powers of life, is
compelled to refund its constitutent matter to the
planet—itself an atom in the universe—on which it
grew.

“ The other, on the contrary, elevates my worth as an
intelligence, even without a limit, and that through
my personality, in which the moral law reveals the
faculty of life independent of my animal nature—nay,
of the.whole material world—at least, if it be permitted
to infer as much from the regulation of being, which
a conformity with that law enacts, proposing, as it
does, my moral worth for the absolute end of my acti-
vity, conceding no surrender of its imperative to a
nocossitation of nature, and spurning in its infinity the
conditions and boundaries of my present transitory
life.””

But we Ivish have always loved philosophy and meta-
physics, whilst Kant reviled it as a so-called science with
all the richness of metaphor which his imagination
could supply. “It was a stormy ocean in which nothing
could be discovered but fog and mist. Te called it a
Serbonian Morass in which man could neither sink nor
swim. It is a shadowy Walhalla which was the ever-
lasting battlefield of shades. It was a babel of confu-
sion in which men, like the builders on the plains of
Shinar, strove in vain to build there a city and a tower
which might reach to Heaven. In fact, according to
Kant, the greatest, if not the only, use of a philosophy
of pure reason was to be found in its purely negative
character—in the protection which its very negation of
knowledge supplies to our practical beliefs in freedom,
immortality, and God.”’ ;

It would bo contrary to the traditions of our race if
wo did not: take an intense interest in present day philo-

ph)f, for even in our darkest days it was held in
‘honour, and as Professor Webb says:— :
¥ Ireland may claim the distinction of having pro-

epoch in the history of thought. Johannes Scotus Eri-

gena, the founder of the Scholastic System—Iutcheson,

plicitly maintained a Theory of Absolute Idealism—

were all men of Irish birth, and were marked, in a

greater or less degree, by the peculiar characteristics of

Irish genius.

At “It has frequently been observed that the genius of
sy the Irish people is naturally borne to dialectics.
i  The author of Hudibras, indeed, selects ¢ the wild
Irish’ as the types of that mystic learning and occult
philosophy which he ridicules in Ralpho. Nor was this
the mere fancy of the poet. As carly as the time of
Charles the Bold, the contemporary chronicler speaks
| of the multitude of philosophers who, like Scotus,
crossed the sea from Ireland. At a later period Bayle
speaks of the Hibernians as renowned for able logicians
and metaphysicians ; and Stewart describes them as dis-
tinguished in all the Continental Universities for their
proficiency in the scholastic logic. And the facts jus-
tify the statement. It was to the uncouth ‘ Hibernian
Figures’ who prowled about the halls of Oviedo that

“Tt is an Irish tutor whom Bayle selects as the man

THE “NATIONAL” BOARD AND IRISH.

N a National School the ordinary hours are usually
from 10 or 10.30 a.m. to 3 p.m., and during this
time certain subjects—viz., Reading, Writing, Spelling,
Arithmetic, Composition, Grammar, Drawing, Singing,
Drill, Kindergarten, Elementary Science, and in girls’
schools, Needlework—must be taught. These “obliga-
tory subjects ’’ are so numerous that teachers find it ox-
tremely difficult to make room for optional subjects,
 such as Irish. The Commissioners’ Report for 1904,
which has just been published, shows, however, that
" Trish, as a subject taught during *ordinary school
* hours,”” has been making steady progress. Irom the
3 ort we take the following :— 3
Refn 1899 TIrish was taught in only- 106 National
~ Sehools. : \
 Tn 1900 the number was still lower, being only 88.
Tn 1901 managers and teachers seem to have
wakened to the importance of the National language,
for we find that Trish was taught in 1,198 schoo]ls.
“In 1902 the number of schools was 1,586, and in 1903
ed its maximum, being taught in 2,018 National

04 ‘shows a slight falling off, the number
n which Irish was taught bcing 1,983.

THE LEADER.

- SEPTEMBER 23, 1905.

From Page 90 of the Report we learn that the pupils
who were taught Irish in these 1,983 schools numbered
95,483, and 1t is satisfactory to see that this number
embraces pupils in all standards, from the lowest to the
highest. ~ It is interesting to compare Irish with the
other languages taught in the National Schools—viz.,
English, French, and Latin. English, being an “obli-
gatory subject,’”’ was taught in all the 8,574 schools in
operation, and the number of pupils who received in-
struction in it was 730,417 ; Irish was taught in 1,983
schools to 95,483 pupils; French in 205 schools to 2,910
pupils, and Latin in 101 schools to 649 pupils. 2

Irish, French, and Latin may be taught not only dur-
ing ordinary schools hours—viz., from 10 or 10.30 to 3—
but also as “ special subjects *’ before or after « ordinary
school hours.”” For feaching a subject during the ordi-
nary school hours there is no special fee, as a teacher’s
class salary and allowances pay him for his work during
this time; but for subjects taught before or after ordi-
nary school hours, a competent teacher can claim ten
shillings for each pupil who passes in Irish, and five shil-
lings for each pupil who passes in French or in Latin.
During the year 1904 the teachers in 1,185 schoo!
taught Irish as a “special ”’ or extra subject, and t}
fees paid to them amounted to £12,069, which shows
that over twenty-four thousand pupils (24,000) must
have “passed ”’ successfully in Irish. For French, the
numbers of schools was 48, and the fees earned amounted
to £137 11s. 6d. ; and for Latin, the corresponding num-
bers were 16 schools and £30 11s. 3d.

The sum £12,069 paid for the teaching of Irish as a
“ special ”’ subject during 1904 is a satisfactory increase
on the amount for 1903, which was £7,220, and this
again was an increase on the amount for 1902, which
was only £3,050.

It is evident from what we have said that Irish has
been making steady progress in the National Schools,
and there was reason to hope that this progress would
continue. From an educational point of view, the pro-
gress of Irish was eminently satisfactory; thousands of
pupils were learning ““a second language *’ at a trifling
cost to the State. But the ways of Governments and of
Government departments are mysterious. From the be-
ginning many members of the so-called “ National ”’
Board looked on the progress of the National language
with an evil eye, and they set about checking it. “Spe-
cial ’ subjects such as Irish are paid for only when
taught to Senior Standards—all Standards below 4th
being excluded. Educationally this is an absurd rule,
but it was not considered enough. The Commissioners
soon added a rule that unless a school was marked as
“good’’ by the inspector in English subjects, no fee
should be paid for Irish. Educationally, this was even
more indefensible than the rule just mentioned, and
after some time it was set aside. But the Treasury and
the Commissioners, who are opposed to Irish, did not
cease their efforts to check the unwelcome progress of
TIrish in the “National’’ Schools, and it has recently been
announced that after 30th June, 1906, no fees what-
ever will be paid for Irish. Plainly, the Treasury and
the Commissioners, who are hostile to Irish, expect that
when fees cease to be paid Irish will cease to be taught
as an extra subject, and that when it ceases to be taught
as an “extra,” it will soon be either wholly given up
during “ordinary school hours,”’ or merely taught in a
perfunctory manner. e

Here again we are back to first principles; our gover-
nors, acting through the so-called “ National’’ Board,
wish that we should not learn Irish, because they know
that it is “ part and paxrcel ’’ of our existence as a na-
tion; and by abolishing fees for Irish they have struck
a heavy blow. The question for the nation is—what
shall be done to' countoract the effects of the action of
the Board and the Treasury? 5

IRISH SEASIDE RESORTS.

P' ITHERTO people in Ireland with limited incomes

have had a natural disinclination to taking a
holiday and consequent change of air by reason of the
fact that accommodation in Irish sea-side resorts resolves
itself into stuffy, uncared-for lodging-houses and expen-
sive hotels. The happy medium was a thing deplored,
but not remedied, until a recently-formed company
started a house at No. 33 The Strand, Kilkee, to be fol-
lowed by similar ventures at every sea-side and pleasure
resort in Ireland.

The house is pleasantly situated, facing the bay,
where one can derive full benefit from the ozone of the
Atlantic without going out of doors. There are three
reception-rooms, communicating by folding-doors, and
these, as well as the bedrooms, five of which face the sea,
aro bright, airy, lofty, and spotlessly clean. Break-
fast—a substantial one is served at 9 a.m., meat lunch
at 2 p.m., and dinner, of three or four courses, at 7
p-m., with coffee and biscuits later on. Any lover of
tea may have it in the afternoon for the mere asking.
The terms aro absurd considering the advantages—some-
thing, T believe, about 30s. a week, with 25 per cent.
reduction for Gaels, the first being, I fancy, what the
proprietress termed “ Empire charges.’’

There is an air of quiet refinement about the place,
while all the time it is as genuinely Irish as the turf-fire
which glowed in the drawing-room grate on cold or rainy
days. It should prove a veritable boon to people, who,
like myself, like home comforts combined with quietness
and geniality, for most of the second-class hotels at

I!l'ls_'h sea-side resorts are noisy and ill-kept—tho eternal
Plain-set being pounded by a favoured few until the
small hours of the morning, which gives the health-
geakgr small chance of gaining his or her quest, and the
inevitable bar drawing undesirable mon and often wo-
men to disturb what peace is left. .

The multiplication of such boarding houses as that at
Kilkee should give a great impetus to Ivish Ireland, for
there Gael may hope to meet his fellow-Gael, and enjoy
& community of interest and thought without the carp-
ing and sneering that one still meets from West Britons
throughout the country, who, for lack of brains, or hope
of gain, still cry down the tongue of their fathers.

Let us hope every good luck and prosperity will follow
the venture started in the Banner County. N.F.D.
